question: 2. A uniform wire is bent into a form coinciding with the portion of the curve \( y=e^{x}, 0 \leq x \leq a, a>1 \), and the line segment \( a-1 \leq x \leq a, y= \) \( e^{a} \). The wire is then suspended from the point \( \left(a-1, e^{a}\right) \) and a horizontal force \( F \) is applied at the point \( (0,1) \) to hold the wire in coincidence with the curve and segment. Assuming the \( \boldsymbol{x} \) axis is horizontal, show that the force \( F \) is directed to the right.
solution:
Solution. The following three statements are evidently equivalent.
The force \( F \) is directed to the right.
The moment of the force of gravity on the wire is clockwise about the point of support.

The centroid of the wire falls to the right of the point of support.
Hence if \( \bar{x} \) is the \( x \)-coordinate of the centroid of the wire, we must prove that
\[
\bar{x}>a-1
\]

Without loss of generality, we may take the constant linear density of the wire to be 1 . Then the mass of the curved portion of the wire is
\[
\int_{0}^{a} \sqrt{1+e^{2 x}} d x
\]
and its \( x \)-moment is
\[
\int_{0}^{u} x \sqrt{1+e^{2 x}} d x .
\]

The straight segment of the wire has mass 1 and \( x \)-moment \( a-\frac{1}{2} \). Hence (1) is equivalent to
\[
a-\frac{1}{2}+\int_{0}^{a} x \sqrt{1+e^{2 x}} d x>(a-1)\left(1+\int_{0}^{a} \sqrt{1+e^{2 x}} d x\right)
\]
which is in turn equivalent to
\[
\int_{0}^{a} x \sqrt{1+e^{2 x}} d x-(a-1) \int_{0}^{a} \sqrt{1+e^{2 x}} d x>-\frac{1}{2}
\]

So we must prove (2) for all \( a>1 \).
Let \( f(a) \) be given by the left member of (2). Then
\[
f^{\prime}(a)=\sqrt{1+e^{2 a}}-\int_{0}^{a} \sqrt{1+e^{2 x}} d x
\]

Since \( 1+e^{2 x}<\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-x}+e^{x}\right)^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{a} \sqrt{1+e^{2 x}} d x & <\int_{0}^{a}\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-x}+e^{x}\right) d x \\
& =e^{a}-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2} e^{-a}<e^{u}<\sqrt{1+e^{2 u}}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( a>0 \). Hence \( f^{\prime}(a)>0 \) for \( a>0 \). This implies \( f(a)>f(0)=0 \) for \( a>0 \). Then (2) follows immediately and we are done.

descriptive_long.question: 2. A uniform wire is bent into a form coinciding with the portion of the curve \( ordinate=e^{abscissa}, 0 \leq abscissa \leq curveend, curveend>1 \), and the line segment \( curveend-1 \leq abscissa \leq curveend, ordinate= \) \( e^{curveend} \). The wire is then suspended from the point \( \left(curveend-1, e^{curveend}\right) \) and a horizontal force \( holdforce \) is applied at the point \( (0,1) \) to hold the wire in coincidence with the curve and segment. Assuming the \( \boldsymbol{abscissa} \) axis is horizontal, show that the force \( holdforce \) is directed to the right.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The following three statements are evidently equivalent.
The force \( holdforce \) is directed to the right.
The moment of the force of gravity on the wire is clockwise about the point of support.

The centroid of the wire falls to the right of the point of support.
Hence if \( \bar{abscissa} \) is the \( abscissa \)-coordinate of the centroid of the wire, we must prove that
\[
\bar{abscissa}>curveend-1
\]

Without loss of generality, we may take the constant linear density of the wire to be 1. Then the mass of the curved portion of the wire is
\[
\int_{0}^{curveend} \sqrt{1+e^{2 abscissa}} \, d abscissa
\]
and its \( abscissa \)-moment is
\[
\int_{0}^{auxiliary} abscissa \sqrt{1+e^{2 abscissa}} \, d abscissa .
\]

The straight segment of the wire has mass 1 and \( abscissa \)-moment \( curveend-\frac{1}{2} \). Hence (1) is equivalent to
\[
curveend-\frac{1}{2}+\int_{0}^{curveend} abscissa \sqrt{1+e^{2 abscissa}} \, d abscissa>(curveend-1)\left(1+\int_{0}^{curveend} \sqrt{1+e^{2 abscissa}} \, d abscissa\right)
\]
which is in turn equivalent to
\[
\int_{0}^{curveend} abscissa \sqrt{1+e^{2 abscissa}} \, d abscissa-(curveend-1) \int_{0}^{curveend} \sqrt{1+e^{2 abscissa}} \, d abscissa>-\frac{1}{2}
\]

So we must prove (2) for all \( curveend>1 \).
Let \( centroid(curveend) \) be given by the left member of (2). Then
\[
centroid^{\prime}(curveend)=\sqrt{1+e^{2 curveend}}-\int_{0}^{curveend} \sqrt{1+e^{2 abscissa}} \, d abscissa
\]

Since \( 1+e^{2 abscissa}<\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-abscissa}+e^{abscissa}\right)^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{curveend} \sqrt{1+e^{2 abscissa}} \, d abscissa & <\int_{0}^{curveend}\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-abscissa}+e^{abscissa}\right) \, d abscissa \\
& =e^{curveend}-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2} e^{-curveend}<e^{auxiliary}<\sqrt{1+e^{2 auxiliary}}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( curveend>0 \). Hence \( centroid^{\prime}(curveend)>0 \) for \( curveend>0 \). This implies \( centroid(curveend)>centroid(0)=0 \) for \( curveend>0 \). Then (2) follows immediately and we are done.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 2. A uniform wire is bent into a form coinciding with the portion of the curve \( raincloud=e^{shoreline}, 0 \leq shoreline \leq blueberry, blueberry>1 \), and the line segment \( blueberry-1 \leq shoreline \leq blueberry, raincloud= \) \( e^{blueberry} \). The wire is then suspended from the point \( \left(blueberry-1, e^{blueberry}\right) \) and a horizontal force \( goldbrick \) is applied at the point \( (0,1) \) to hold the wire in coincidence with the curve and segment. Assuming the \( \boldsymbol{shoreline} \) axis is horizontal, show that the force \( goldbrick \) is directed to the right.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The following three statements are evidently equivalent.
The force \( goldbrick \) is directed to the right.
The moment of the force of gravity on the wire is clockwise about the point of support.

The centroid of the wire falls to the right of the point of support.
Hence if \( \bar{shoreline} \) is the \( shoreline \)-coordinate of the centroid of the wire, we must prove that
\[
\bar{shoreline}>blueberry-1
\]

Without loss of generality, we may take the constant linear density of the wire to be 1 . Then the mass of the curved portion of the wire is
\[
\int_{0}^{blueberry} \sqrt{1+e^{2 shoreline}} d shoreline
\]
and its \( shoreline \)-moment is
\[
\int_{0}^{windstorm} shoreline \sqrt{1+e^{2 shoreline}} d shoreline .
\]

The straight segment of the wire has mass 1 and \( shoreline \)-moment \( blueberry-\frac{1}{2} \). Hence (1) is equivalent to
\[
blueberry-\frac{1}{2}+\int_{0}^{blueberry} shoreline \sqrt{1+e^{2 shoreline}} d shoreline>(blueberry-1)\left(1+\int_{0}^{blueberry} \sqrt{1+e^{2 shoreline}} d shoreline\right)
\]
which is in turn equivalent to
\[
\int_{0}^{blueberry} shoreline \sqrt{1+e^{2 shoreline}} d shoreline-(blueberry-1) \int_{0}^{blueberry} \sqrt{1+e^{2 shoreline}} d shoreline>-\frac{1}{2}
\]

So we must prove (2) for all \( blueberry>1 \).
Let \( stoneware(blueberry) \) be given by the left member of (2). Then
\[
stoneware^{\prime}(blueberry)=\sqrt{1+e^{2 blueberry}}-\int_{0}^{blueberry} \sqrt{1+e^{2 shoreline}} d shoreline
\]

Since \( 1+e^{2 shoreline}<\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-shoreline}+e^{shoreline}\right)^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{blueberry} \sqrt{1+e^{2 shoreline}} d shoreline & <\int_{0}^{blueberry}\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-shoreline}+e^{shoreline}\right) d shoreline \\
& =e^{blueberry}-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2} e^{-blueberry}<e^{windstorm}<\sqrt{1+e^{2 windstorm}}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( blueberry>0 \). Hence \( stoneware^{\prime}(blueberry)>0 \) for \( blueberry>0 \). This implies \( stoneware(blueberry)>stoneware(0)=0 \) for \( blueberry>0 \). Then (2) follows immediately and we are done.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 2. A uniform wire is bent into a form coinciding with the portion of the curve \( abscissa=e^{verticalaxis}, 0 \leq verticalaxis \leq minuscule, minuscule>1 \), and the line segment \( minuscule-1 \leq verticalaxis \leq minuscule, abscissa= e^{minuscule} \). The wire is then suspended from the point \( (minuscule-1, e^{minuscule}) \) and a horizontal force \( stillness \) is applied at the point \( (0,1) \) to hold the wire in coincidence with the curve and segment. Assuming the \( \boldsymbol{verticalaxis} \) axis is horizontal, show that the force \( stillness \) is directed to the right.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The following three statements are evidently equivalent.
The force \( stillness \) is directed to the right.
The moment of the force of gravity on the wire is clockwise about the point of support.

The centroid of the wire falls to the right of the point of support.
Hence if \( \bar{verticalaxis} \) is the \( verticalaxis \)-coordinate of the centroid of the wire, we must prove that
\[
\bar{verticalaxis}>minuscule-1
\]

Without loss of generality, we may take the constant linear density of the wire to be 1 . Then the mass of the curved portion of the wire is
\[
\int_{0}^{minuscule} \sqrt{1+e^{2 verticalaxis}} d verticalaxis
\]
and its \( verticalaxis \)-moment is
\[
\int_{0}^{knownvalue} verticalaxis \sqrt{1+e^{2 verticalaxis}} d verticalaxis .
\]

The straight segment of the wire has mass 1 and \( verticalaxis \)-moment \( minuscule-\frac{1}{2} \). Hence (1) is equivalent to
\[
minuscule-\frac{1}{2}+\int_{0}^{minuscule} verticalaxis \sqrt{1+e^{2 verticalaxis}} d verticalaxis>(minuscule-1)\left(1+\int_{0}^{minuscule} \sqrt{1+e^{2 verticalaxis}} d verticalaxis\right)
\]
which is in turn equivalent to
\[
\int_{0}^{minuscule} verticalaxis \sqrt{1+e^{2 verticalaxis}} d verticalaxis-(minuscule-1) \int_{0}^{minuscule} \sqrt{1+e^{2 verticalaxis}} d verticalaxis>-\frac{1}{2}
\]

So we must prove (2) for all \( minuscule>1 \).
Let \( constant(minuscule) \) be given by the left member of (2). Then
\[
constant^{\prime}(minuscule)=\sqrt{1+e^{2 minuscule}}-\int_{0}^{minuscule} \sqrt{1+e^{2 verticalaxis}} d verticalaxis
\]

Since \( 1+e^{2 verticalaxis}<\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-verticalaxis}+e^{verticalaxis}\right)^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{minuscule} \sqrt{1+e^{2 verticalaxis}} d verticalaxis & <\int_{0}^{minuscule}\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-verticalaxis}+e^{verticalaxis}\right) d verticalaxis \\
& =e^{minuscule}-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2} e^{-minuscule}<e^{knownvalue}<\sqrt{1+e^{2 knownvalue}}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( minuscule>0 \). Hence \( constant^{\prime}(minuscule)>0 \) for \( minuscule>0 \). This implies \( constant(minuscule)>constant(0)=0 \) for \( minuscule>0 \). Then (2) follows immediately and we are done.

garbled_string.question: 2. A uniform wire is bent into a form coinciding with the portion of the curve \( hjgrksla=e^{qzxwvtnp}, 0 \leq qzxwvtnp \leq vfyrsplk, vfyrsplk>1 \), and the line segment \( vfyrsplk-1 \leq qzxwvtnp \leq vfyrsplk, hjgrksla= e^{vfyrsplk} \). The wire is then suspended from the point \( \left(vfyrsplk-1, e^{vfyrsplk}\right) \) and a horizontal force kzmpxwhr is applied at the point \( (0,1) \) to hold the wire in coincidence with the curve and segment. Assuming the \( \boldsymbol{qzxwvtnp} \) axis is horizontal, show that the force kzmpxwhr is directed to the right.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The following three statements are evidently equivalent.
The force \( kzmpxwhr \) is directed to the right.
The moment of the force of gravity on the wire is clockwise about the point of support.

The centroid of the wire falls to the right of the point of support.
Hence if \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \) is the \( qzxwvtnp \)-coordinate of the centroid of the wire, we must prove that
\[
\bar{qzxwvtnp}>vfyrsplk-1
\]

Without loss of generality, we may take the constant linear density of the wire to be 1 . Then the mass of the curved portion of the wire is
\[
\int_{0}^{vfyrsplk} \sqrt{1+e^{2 qzxwvtnp}} d qzxwvtnp
\]
and its \( qzxwvtnp \)-moment is
\[
\int_{0}^{bnecsotq} qzxwvtnp \sqrt{1+e^{2 qzxwvtnp}} d qzxwvtnp .
\]

The straight segment of the wire has mass 1 and \( qzxwvtnp \)-moment \( vfyrsplk-\frac{1}{2} \). Hence (1) is equivalent to
\[
vfyrsplk-\frac{1}{2}+\int_{0}^{vfyrsplk} qzxwvtnp \sqrt{1+e^{2 qzxwvtnp}} d qzxwvtnp>(vfyrsplk-1)\left(1+\int_{0}^{vfyrsplk} \sqrt{1+e^{2 qzxwvtnp}} d qzxwvtnp\right)
\]
which is in turn equivalent to
\[
\int_{0}^{vfyrsplk} qzxwvtnp \sqrt{1+e^{2 qzxwvtnp}} d qzxwvtnp-(vfyrsplk-1) \int_{0}^{vfyrsplk} \sqrt{1+e^{2 qzxwvtnp}} d qzxwvtnp>-\frac{1}{2}
\]

So we must prove (2) for all \( vfyrsplk>1 \).
Let \( rdtqklvn(vfyrsplk) \) be given by the left member of (2). Then
\[
rdtqklvn^{\prime}(vfyrsplk)=\sqrt{1+e^{2 vfyrsplk}}-\int_{0}^{vfyrsplk} \sqrt{1+e^{2 qzxwvtnp}} d qzxwvtnp
\]

Since \( 1+e^{2 qzxwvtnp}<\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-qzxwvtnp}+e^{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{vfyrsplk} \sqrt{1+e^{2 qzxwvtnp}} d qzxwvtnp & <\int_{0}^{vfyrsplk}\left(\frac{1}{2} e^{-qzxwvtnp}+e^{qzxwvtnp}\right) d qzxwvtnp \\
& =e^{vfyrsplk}-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2} e^{-vfyrsplk}<e^{bnecsotq}<\sqrt{1+e^{2 bnecsotq}}
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( vfyrsplk>0 \). Hence \( rdtqklvn^{\prime}(vfyrsplk)>0 \) for \( vfyrsplk>0 \). This implies \( rdtqklvn(vfyrsplk)>rdtqklvn(0)=0 \) for \( vfyrsplk>0 \). Then (2) follows immediately and we are done.

kernel_variant.question:
Let a > 3 be fixed and let  
 0 < R < e^a - 1, \lambda  > 0  
be real constants.  A homogeneous wire of linear mass-density \lambda  is bent so that it coincides with the planar curve \Gamma  obtained by joining consecutively  

 C_1 (exponential branch) r_1(x) = (x , ex , 0)          0 \leq  x \leq  a,

 C_2 (vertical segment)  r_2(t) = (a , e^a - t , 0)        0 \leq  t \leq  R,

 C_3 (horizontal segment) r_3(s) = (a - s , e^a - R , 0)     0 \leq  s \leq  3.

Thus \Gamma  lies in the plane z = 0 and terminates at  

 S = (a - 3 , e^a - R , 0).

The wire is freely suspended at S and placed in a uniform gravitational field  
 g = -g_0 j (g_0 > 0).  
A single horizontal force F, applied at  

 Q = (0 , 1 , 0),

keeps the wire in static equilibrium.  Using the right-handed orthonormal basis (i, j, k) whose i-axis points to the right, prove that the balancing force must point to the right, i.e.  

 F\cdot i > 0 for every choice of parameters a > 3 and 0 < R < e^a - 1.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout set  
 g(x) := \sqrt{1 + e^{2x}}, I(a) := \int _0^a g(x) dx, J(a) := \int _0^a x g(x) dx,           (1)  
and denote by (x, y, 0) the centre of mass of the whole wire, whose total mass is M.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
1.  Torque balance and the role of x  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Because \Gamma  lies in the plane z = 0, every mass element has position vector  

 r = (x - S_x) i + (y - S_y) j.

Its infinitesimal weight is -\lambda g_0 j ds, so  

 d\tau _G = r \times  (-\lambda g_0 j) = -\lambda g_0(x - S_x) k.

Integrating over \Gamma  gives the total gravitational torque  

 \tau _G = -Mg_0(x - S_x) k.                                              (2)

The applied force is horizontal, F = (F_x, 0, 0).  Its moment about the suspension point S is  

 \tau _F = (Q - S) \times  F = -(1 - S_y) F_x k.                            (3)

Because 0 < R < e^a - 1 we have S_y = e^a - R > 1, hence 1 - S_y < 0.  Thus \tau _F and F_x always have the same sign.  Static equilibrium (\tau _F + \tau _G = 0) implies  

 sgn F_x = sgn (x - S_x).                                            (4)

Consequently it suffices to prove  

 x > S_x = a - 3.                                                    (5)

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
2.  Masses and first moments of the three pieces  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  

C_1 (0 \leq  x \leq  a):            M_1 = \lambda  I(a),   M_1x = \lambda  J(a).  
C_2 (vertical, length R):    M_2 = \lambda R,        M_2x = \lambda aR.  
C_3 (horizontal, length 3):  M_3 = 3\lambda ,       M_3x = \lambda \int _{a-3}^{a} x dx = 3\lambda (a - 1.5).

Hence  

 M  = \lambda [ I(a) + R + 3 ],                                            (6)  

 Mx = \lambda [ J(a) + aR + 3(a - 1.5) ].                                 (7)

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
3.  Reduction to a single integral inequality  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Define  

 \Delta  := Mx - (a - 3)M = \lambda { J(a) - (a - 3)I(a) + 3R + 4.5 }.          (8)

Because R > 0, the last two terms are strictly positive.  Therefore (5) will follow once we have proved  

 f(a) := J(a) - (a - 3)I(a) > 0 for all a > 3.                    (9)

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
4.  A differential criterion for f(a)  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Differentiate:

 f '(a) = a g(a) - [(a - 3) g(a) + I(a)] = 3 g(a) - I(a).         (10)

Thus f is strictly increasing whenever I(a) < 3 g(a).  We now prove this estimate for every a > 3.

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
5.  Bounding I(a) in terms of g(a)  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
For x \leq  a the elementary inequality e^{x} \leq  e^{a} gives  

 g(x) = \sqrt{1 + e^{2x}} \leq  e^{x - a} g(a) + 1.                        (11)

Integrating (11) from 0 to a yields  

 I(a) \leq  g(a)\int _0^a e^{x - a}dx + a  
      = g(a)(1 - e^{-a}) + a.                                      (12)

Divide by g(a) and use g(a) \geq  e^{a}:

 I(a)/g(a) \leq  1 - e^{-a} + a/e^{a}.                                (13)

For a \geq  3 the right-hand side is at most

 1 - e^{-3} + 3/e^{3} \approx  1 - 0.0498 + 0.1494 < 1.1.                (14)

Hence  

 I(a) < 1.1 g(a) < 3 g(a) for all a > 3,                          (15)

and from (10) we obtain  

 f '(a) > 0 for all a > 3.                                        (16)

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
6.  Positivity of f(a)  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Because f is increasing and  

 f(3) = J(3) > 0,                                                  (17)

we have f(a) > 0 for every a > 3.  Returning to (8) we conclude  

 \Delta  > 0 \Rightarrow  Mx > (a - 3)M \Rightarrow  x > a - 3.                              (18)

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
7.  Conclusion  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Combining (4) and (18) we deduce F_x > 0, i.e.  

 F\cdot i > 0.

Thus the horizontal force required to keep the wire in equilibrium must always be directed to the right.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Additional geometry:  The wire now lives in ℝ³ and contains a curved
 3-dimensional piece (the quarter-circle C₂); torque must be handled with full
 vector machinery rather than planar moments.

• More segments:  Three distinct pieces (one nonlinear in each coordinate)
 interact; the planar problem had just two.

• Non-trivial arc length:  The exponential branch requires integration of
 √(1+e^{2x}), while the arc length of C₂ is πR/2.  Although the latter drops out
 of the final inequality, recognising this fact is part of the challenge.

• Inequality sharpening:  Showing f′(a) > 0 needs an effective
 estimate on ∫₀ᵃ g(x) dx relative to g(a); a naïve bound
 ∫≤a g(a) would fail when a > 3, so one must craft a sharper argument using the
 specific growth of g.

• Torque in three dimensions:  Students must translate the “centroid to the
 right” idea into a 3-D moment calculation and keep track of vector directions
 (î, ĵ, k̂).

Together these points raise the algebraic, geometric, and conceptual load well
beyond that of the original two-piece planar question.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let a > 3 be fixed and let  
 0 < R < e^a - 1, \lambda  > 0  
be real constants.  A homogeneous wire of linear mass-density \lambda  is bent so that it coincides with the planar curve \Gamma  obtained by joining consecutively

----------------------------------------------------------------------

C_2 (vertical, length R):    M_2 = \lambda R,        M_2x = \lambda aR.  
C_3 (horizontal, length 3):  M_3 = 3\lambda ,       M_3x = \lambda \int _{a-3}^{a} x dx = 3\lambda (a - 1.5).

Hence

 Mx = \lambda [ J(a) + aR + 3(a - 1.5) ].                                 (7)

----------------------------------------------------------------------

 f(a) := J(a) - (a - 3)I(a) > 0 for all a > 3.                    (9)

----------------------------------------------------------------------

Thus f is strictly increasing whenever I(a) < 3 g(a).  We now prove this estimate for every a > 3.

----------------------------------------------------------------------

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
For x \leq  a the elementary inequality e^{x} \leq  e^{a} gives  

 g(x) = \sqrt{1 + e^{2x}} \leq  e^{x - a} g(a) + 1.                        (11)

Integrating (11) from 0 to a yields  

 I(a) \leq  g(a)\int _0^a e^{x - a}dx + a  
      = g(a)(1 - e^{-a}) + a.                                      (12)

Divide by g(a) and use g(a) \geq  e^{a}:

 I(a)/g(a) \leq  1 - e^{-a} + a/e^{a}.                                (13)

For a \geq  3 the right-hand side is at most

 1 - e^{-3} + 3/e^{3} \approx  1 - 0.0498 + 0.1494 < 1.1.                (14)

Hence  

 I(a) < 1.1 g(a) < 3 g(a) for all a > 3,                          (15)

and from (10) we obtain  

 f '(a) > 0 for all a > 3.                                        (16)

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
6.  Positivity of f(a)  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Because f is increasing and  

 f(3) = J(3) > 0,                                                  (17)

we have f(a) > 0 for every a > 3.  Returning to (8) we conclude  

 \Delta  > 0 \Rightarrow  Mx > (a - 3)M \Rightarrow  x > a - 3.                              (18)

----------------------------------------------------------------------  
7.  Conclusion  
----------------------------------------------------------------------  
Combining (4) and (18) we deduce F_x > 0, i.e.  

 F\cdot i > 0.

Thus the horizontal force required to keep the wire in equilibrium must always be directed to the right.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Additional geometry:  The wire now lives in ℝ³ and contains a curved
 3-dimensional piece (the quarter-circle C₂); torque must be handled with full
 vector machinery rather than planar moments.

• More segments:  Three distinct pieces (one nonlinear in each coordinate)
 interact; the planar problem had just two.

• Non-trivial arc length:  The exponential branch requires integration of
 √(1+e^{2x}), while the arc length of C₂ is πR/2.  Although the latter drops out
 of the final inequality, recognising this fact is part of the challenge.

• Inequality sharpening:  Showing f′(a) > 0 needs an effective
 estimate on ∫₀ᵃ g(x) dx relative to g(a); a naïve bound
 ∫≤a g(a) would fail when a > 3, so one must craft a sharper argument using the
 specific growth of g.

• Torque in three dimensions:  Students must translate the “centroid to the
 right” idea into a 3-D moment calculation and keep track of vector directions
 (î, ĵ, k̂).

Together these points raise the algebraic, geometric, and conceptual load well
beyond that of the original two-piece planar question.
